topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored envelope sat on my cluttered desk, an anomaly amidst the stacks of overdue bills and forgotten manuscripts. My fingers, usually steady when typing, trembled slightly as I reached for it. The handwriting on the front was familiar, elegant yet imbued with a deep, undeniable remorse. It was from Mark. Years. Years had passed since the disastrous night at the gallery opening, the night everything fractured between us. I pulled the letter out, the paper feeling impossibly fragile under my touch. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that seemed to catch in my throat. I reread the opening lines, the carefully chosen words swimming before my eyes. A strange, heavy pressure settled in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I looked around my quiet apartment, the familiar surroundings suddenly feeling alien and too bright. I didn't move to put it down; I just stared, my gaze fixed on the ink, waiting for something to shift, something to make sense of this unexpected arrival.

[story 2]

I found the letter tucked inside an old book I’d meant to donate. It was addressed to me, dated nearly five years after the argument that had ended our friendship. When I finally unfolded the paper, the world seemed to slow down. My heart began to pound a frantic rhythm against my ribs, loud enough that I worried anyone nearby could hear it. I reread the script, my eyes tracing the loops and flourishes of his penmanship. A coldness washed over me, a sudden draining of warmth. I dropped the letter onto the wooden floor, the sound echoing unnervingly in the silence of the room. I stood up abruptly, pacing a tight circle in front of the window, my hands clenching and unclenching at my sides. My vision blurred momentarily, and I blinked hard, trying to clear the sudden fog that had descended upon my mind.

[story 3]

The mail slot rattled, and I retrieved the thick, sealed envelope. It was Mark’s handwriting, unmistakable, and the date confirmed it was from the night of the incident, years removed. As I broke the seal, the scent of aged paper mingled with something sharp and metallic—a scent that instantly pulled me backward. My entire body went rigid. I couldn't seem to process the words; they swam on the page, indistinct and overwhelming. I sat down heavily on the edge of my sofa, my posture stiff, every muscle taut. I kept glancing at the letter, unable to look away, my jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. A strange tremor ran through my hands, and I found myself gripping the fabric of the cushion until my knuckles were white. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming inside my own chest.
